metadata.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Priority Queue (Heap), Recursion Basics, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine an online gaming leaderboard where millions of players have scores. Instead of sorting every score, you only need to know who has the kth highest score.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine an online gaming leaderboard where millions of players have scores. Instead of sorting every score, you only need to know who has the kth highest score.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Min Heap pattern whenever you need the kth largest/smallest element without fully sorting the array.
learning.signals: Whenever the problem asks for kth largest/smallest or Top K elements without requiring complete sorting, think Min Heap or Quickselect.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest element in sorted order, not the kth distinct element.

examples.explanation: The sorted array is [6,5,4,3,2,1]. The 2nd largest element is 5.
After sorting in descending order, the 4th largest element is 4.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Sort the array in descending order and return the kth element.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: We only care about the largest k elements. Maintain a Min Heap of size k. Whenever the heap grows larger than k, remove the smallest element. At the end, the heap's top is the kth largest element.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Keep only the largest k elements using a Min Heap.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever the problem asks for kth largest/smallest or Top K elements without requiring complete sorting, think Min Heap or Quickselect.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The heap always stores the k largest elements. Every insertion and deletion takes O(log k), making the overall complexity O(n log k), which is much better than sorting when k is much smaller than n.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever the problem asks for kth largest/smallest or Top K elements without requiring complete sorting, think Min Heap or Quickselect.
commonWrongPaths: Confusing kth largest with kth distinct largest.
Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Removing elements before heap size exceeds k.
Returning the last inserted element instead of heap top.

hints: You don't need to sort the entire array.
Keep only the largest k elements.
Use a Min Heap of size k.
commonMisconceptions: Confusing kth largest with kth distinct largest.
Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Removing elements before heap size exceeds k.
Returning the last inserted element instead of heap top.

pattern.name: Min Heap
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever the problem asks for kth largest/smallest or Top K elements without requiring complete sorting, think Min Heap or Quickselect.

approaches.title: Sorting
approaches.idea: Sort the array in descending order and return the kth element.
algorithm: Sort the array in descending order.

approaches.title: Quickselect
approaches.idea: Use partitioning similar to QuickSort to place the kth largest element into its correct position without completely sorting the array.
algorithm: Compare pivot position with target index.
approaches.dryRun: Repeatedly partition until the target index is found.

approaches.title: Min Heap
approaches.idea: Maintain a Min Heap containing only the k largest elements seen so far. If the heap size exceeds k, remove the smallest element. The heap root will always be the kth largest element.
approaches.intuition: The heap always stores the k largest elements. Every insertion and deletion takes O(log k), making the overall complexity O(n log k), which is much better than sorting when k is much smaller than n.
algorithm: If heap size exceeds k, remove the smallest element.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2
Heap=[3]
Heap=[2,3]
Insert 1 -> Remove 1
Heap=[2,3]
Insert 5 -> Remove 2
Heap=[3,5]
Insert 6 -> Remove 3
Heap=[5,6]
Insert 4 -> Remove 4
Heap=[5,6]
Answer=5

code.javascript: JavaScript requires implementing a Min Heap class. The algorithm remains the same: maintain a heap of size k and return its root.

visualization.description: Maintain only the largest k elements inside the heap.

revisionNotes: Remove smallest when size exceeds k.

interviewFollowUps: Can you solve it without sorting?
Why does a Min Heap work?
How would you find the kth smallest element?
How would you process a data stream?
interviewTips: Explain why Min Heap stores only k elements.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Keep only the largest k elements using a Min Heap.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Insert → If size > k remove smallest → Return heap top.

importantPoints: Min Heap stores k largest elements.
commonMistakes: Confusing kth largest with kth distinct largest.
Using a Max Heap instead of a Min Heap.
Removing elements before heap size exceeds k.
Returning the last inserted element instead of heap top.
